narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 40% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. You bring some strong qualifications, like an impressive undergraduate GPA of 3.8—well above the typical student's 3.21—and you're planning to attend full-time, just like most applicants. However, your school performance in Year 1 and Year 3 placed you in the bottom decile (1 out of 10), far below the average of around the middle of the pack, and your LSAT score of 33 is noticeably lower than the usual 36.7. Coming from a middle-income family, as a Black female applicant, you represent a smaller portion of typical applicants, who are more often White males from varied backgrounds. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your Year 3 school decile, which strongly nudged the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure due to its low ranking. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which similarly pushed the odds toward failure because it fell short of what's common for successful students. Your Year 1 school decile ranked third in influence, again contributing to the failure risk with its bottom-decile position. Family income came fourth, with your middle bracket adding a slight push toward higher risk compared to broader distributions. Your racial background ranked fifth, influencing the prediction toward failure in line with lower representation among passers. Even your strong undergraduate GPA, while helpful, had less sway and still tilted slightly toward risk in this context, followed distantly by gender and full-time status. Overall, these top factors—especially your school deciles and LSAT—stood out as bigger drags than your strengths could offset, unlike typical admitted students who excel across early academic markers. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT for a higher score through targeted prep, build a track record of academic improvement in law school if possible, and seek bar exam-specific study resources early. With your solid undergrad foundation, addressing those early weaknesses could shift the model's view dramatically next time. Keep pushing—you've already shown grit with that GPA.
